metadata.title: M87* Observational Data — Event Horizon Telescope and Multi-wavelength Studies
metadata.description: Comprehensive observational database of the supermassive black hole M87* (Virgo A, NGC 4486) from EHT and supporting observations

distance.source: EHT Collaboration Paper I (2019), Bird et al. (2010)

EHT2019.method: EHT shadow size combined with GR models

stellarDynamics_Liepold2023.method: Stellar orbit-superposition models with axisymmetric Jeans modeling

adopted.note: EHT result is consistent with stellar dynamics and provides the most direct measurement

eventHorizonDiameter.comparison: About 2.5 times the diameter of Pluto's orbit

angularSize.note: The ring diameter is consistent with the predicted shadow size for the measured mass

EHTconstraint2023.note: EHT polarimetric observations suggest significant spin

jetsRequireHighSpin.note: The powerful relativistic jet of M87 almost certainly requires a rapidly spinning black hole to power via the Blandford-Znajek mechanism

firstImage.note: Equivalent angular resolution to reading a newspaper in New York from a cafe in Paris

telescopeArray.name: Event Horizon Telescope
correlatedAt: Max Planck Institute for Radio Astronomy, Bonn

brightnessAsymmetry.description: The south side of the ring is brighter than the north
brightnessAsymmetry.cause: Relativistic Doppler beaming from the approaching side of the accretion flow
brightnessAsymmetry.approachingJetSide: south (counterclockwise orbital motion as seen from Earth)

centralBrightnessDepression.description: The interior of the ring (the 'shadow') is significantly dimmer
centralBrightnessDepression.cause: Photon capture by the black hole — the shadow is the black hole's silhouette against the emission

azimuthalStructure.description: The ring brightness varies with azimuthal angle
azimuthalStructure.note: Position angle measured east of north

ringCircularity.note: Consistent with a circular ring to within 10%, constraining the inclination and spin

multiEpochObservations.description: M87* was observed in multiple years showing evolving ring morphology

epochs.result: Compact structure detected, size consistent with shadow
First resolved image — asymmetric bright ring with central depression
Ring structure maintained, brightness peak shifted in position angle
Image quality improved, ring morphology persistent

morphologicalEvolution.description: The ring diameter remains constant across epochs, but the azimuthal brightness distribution changes
morphologicalEvolution.interpretation: The ring size is set by the black hole mass (constant), while the brightness distribution reflects turbulent accretion dynamics
morphologicalEvolution.rotationOfBrightSpot: The position angle of peak brightness rotates over years, consistent with turbulent accretion flow variability

polarimetry.description: First resolved polarimetric image of a black hole shadow

linearPolarizationFraction.note: Up to 15% linear polarization in resolved regions

EVPApattern.description: Electric Vector Position Angle shows a nearly azimuthal pattern around the ring
EVPApattern.interpretation: Indicates a coherent, ordered magnetic field with a significant poloidal component near the event horizon
EVPApattern.note: The ordered spiral EVPA pattern is a signature of MAD (Magnetically Arrested Disk) state

nearHorizon_Gauss.note: Field strength estimated from synchrotron modeling

rotationMeasure_rad_m2.note: Large Faraday rotation measures indicate significant magnetized plasma

implicationsForJetLaunching.description: The polarimetric results strongly support jet launching via the Blandford-Znajek mechanism

polarimetry.source: EHT Collaboration Papers VII and VIII (2021)

jet.note_PA: Position angle of the approaching jet, measured east of north

inclination_deg.note: Nearly pointing toward Earth but not close enough for blazar classification

innerJet.note: Near the core, measured from proper motions

counterjet.note: Counter-jet is much fainter due to Doppler deboosting

openingAngle_deg.note: Jet initially has wide opening angle and collimates into a parabolic shape

accretionRate.note: Highly uncertain; estimated from Faraday rotation measurements and modeling

eddingtonRatio.note: M87* accretes at about one millionth of its Eddington rate

accretionFlowType.note: Low accretion rate means the flow is geometrically thick and optically thin

electronTemperature.description: Electrons are relativistic with kT ~ mec²

ionTemperature.description: Ions are significantly hotter than electrons (two-temperature plasma)

accretionProperties.radiationMechanism: Synchrotron emission from relativistic electrons in magnetic fields

infrared.note: Contribution from both jet and accretion flow

optical.note: Optical emission dominated by synchrotron from jet

xray.note: X-ray emission from jet and hot accretion flow

gammaRay.variability: TeV flares on timescale of days

neutrino.note: No significant neutrino signal associated with M87

GRMHDsimulations.description: General Relativistic Magnetohydrodynamic simulations used to interpret EHT data
GRMHDsimulations.libraryUsed: Comprehensive library of >60,000 GRMHD snapshot images

preferredRhigh.note: Controls electron temperature in disk vs jet

alternativeGravityConstraints.description: EHT observations constrain deviations from Kerr metric

metricDeviationParameter.name: δ (fractional deviation from Kerr shadow size)
metricDeviationParameter.source: Psaltis et al. (2020), EHT Paper VI

noHairTheoremTest.description: Shadow circularity constrains independent multipole moments
noHairTheoremTest.note: Consistent with the Kerr solution (no-hair theorem)

EHTpapers.title: First M87 Event Horizon Telescope Results. I. The Shadow of the Supermassive Black Hole
First M87 Event Horizon Telescope Results. II. Array and Instrumentation
First M87 Event Horizon Telescope Results. III. Data Processing and Calibration
First M87 Event Horizon Telescope Results. IV. Imaging the Central Supermassive Black Hole
First M87 Event Horizon Telescope Results. V. Physical Origin of the Asymmetric Ring
First M87 Event Horizon Telescope Results. VI. The Shadow and Mass of the Central Black Hole
First M87 Event Horizon Telescope Results. VII. Polarization of the Ring
First M87 Event Horizon Telescope Results. VIII. Magnetic Field Structure near The Event Horizon
First M87 Event Horizon Telescope Results. IX. Detection of Near-horizon Circular Polarization

jetDiscovery.description: Observed as a 'curious straight ray' extending from the nucleus

radioSource.discoverers: Hey, Parsons & Phillips; Bolton, Stanley & Slee

superluminalMotion.note: Among first sources where apparent superluminal motion was identified

sources: Event Horizon Telescope Collaboration, ApJL 875, L1-L6 (2019)
Event Horizon Telescope Collaboration, ApJL 910, L12-L13 (2021)
Event Horizon Telescope Collaboration, ApJL 957, L20 (2023)
